channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Donald Trump could be the man to save Cuba
item.description: Ideological certainties have hurt Cubans for 70 years. Time to give Trumpian cynicism a chance

item.title: The Deadly Price of China’s Coal Boom
item.description: Two fatal disasters highlight enduring problems in the mining industry.

item.title: Russia Pounds Ukraine With Missile, Drone Strikes
item.description: Moscow warned of a massive attack on Kyiv. And it delivered.

item.title: The fading influence of America’s spy co-ordinator
item.description: Bill Pulte’s appointment as director of national intelligence reflects the office’s declining stature

item.title: Two Senators Just Blew Up Trump’s Boat-Strike Justifications
item.description: Sens. Kaine and Paul say that the military’s targeting criteria don’t include the presence of drugs or arms.

item.title: The Gulf Crisis Is Forcing Japan Into an Energy Rethink
item.description: Tokyo spent decades preparing for disruption—then found its options narrowing.

item.title: Want to know the future? Don’t trust the stockmarket
item.description: Share prices are buffeted by far more than just new information

item.title: Nvidia wants to supercharge your laptop
item.description: Jensen Huang is bringing the firm’s AI act to the PC

item.title: Britain is wrong to ban speakers like Hasan Piker
item.description: Even though his views are awful

item.title: Can Donald Trump save Israel from itself in Lebanon?
item.description: Binyamin Netanyahu’s fight with Hizbullah is complicating the president’s talks with Iran

item.title: China’s high-tech rise is leaving much of the country behind
item.description: That could make a starkly unequal country even more so

item.title: British Steel Needs a Transformation, Not a Bailout
item.description: The recent revival of industrial policy offers an opportunity to break the old pattern whereby economic risks are socialized while rewards remain privatized. In addition to attaching meaningful conditionalities to public support, governments must invest in the state capacity required to govern strategic sectors well.

item.title: Kazakhstan, Russia Step Forward With $16.4 Billion Nuclear Power Plant Plan
item.description: Moscow is reportedly furnishing as much as 85 percent of the financing for the project.

item.title: How to Encourage Deceased Organ Donation
item.description: Developing countries facing organ shortages often search for complex solutions, such as sweeping legal reforms. But Tunisia’s experience suggests a much simpler first step: awareness-raising sessions where doctors explain how the transplant system works, and people share their fears and concerns.

item.title: Nepal’s Prime Minister Mistakes Mandate for Immunity
item.description: Balendra Shah, who assumed office just two months ago, does as he pleases, with little regard for constitutional norms, parliamentary procedures or diplomatic niceties.

item.title: Building Alliances of America’s Allies
item.description: In a world in which the US cannot be counted on as it once was, the objective is not stability at any price, but rather stability on terms consistent with national and Western interests. This is achievable, but only if the friends of the US recognize the new reality and act individually and collectively to meet the challenge.

item.title: Moscow’s New Military Partner Has Something Russia Needs More Than Allies
item.description: The security provisions in recent agreements with the Taliban may reflect intent, but the more immediate benefit on the Russian side appears to be addressing the labor shortage.

item.title: Beyond China and the US: How Japan is Incrementally Rebalancing Its Foreign Policy
item.description: Neither the Japan-U.S. alliance nor the Sino-Japanese economic relationship will completely unravel overnight. However, the overall trendlines have led Tokyo to seek further hedges.

item.title: Unholy Violence: Weaponizing Religion Against Gender Rights Defenders in Myanmar
item.description: Buddhist fundamentalism functions in post-coup Myanmar not as a parallel force to military authoritarianism, but as an integral component of it.

item.title: Why India Cannot Let the Rupee Float
item.description: Imported inflation, fragile manufacturing, and unequal burdens complicate textbook arguments supporting unrestricted currency depreciation across India.

item.title: Kashmir’s Uneasy Calm: Silent Guns, Muted Hope
item.description: True integration, it appears, will remain elusive until New Delhi stops treating Kashmir as a security variable to be managed.

item.title: The Politics Behind Taiwan’s Soft Power
item.description: “Taiwan Travelogue” and its Booker Prize win highlight the nuances – and controversies – of contemporary culture in Taiwan.

item.title: The Promise and Limits of African Data Sovereignty
item.description: African countries are recognizing that no matter how well-designed their data-protection regulations are, their sovereignty over US-based Big Tech firms ends at the boundary between subsidiary and parent. Overcoming this barrier will require a level of coordination that the continent has yet to achieve.

item.title: Israel Has a Growing Anti-Christian Problem
item.description: A recent attack in Jerusalem highlights a worrying trend intensified by wartime nationalism.

item.title: The AI Economy’s Permanent Underclass
item.description: AI is transforming the global economy while widening the gap between technological winners and losers. Governments that fail to secure a place in the AI supply chain may find themselves confronting mass job displacement without the tax revenues or state capacity needed to contain the social and political fallout.

item.title: The World’s Mineral Powers Seize Their Moment
item.description: Resource-rich countries haven’t always benefited from extraction. Can this time be different?

item.title: Wanted: Asia news editor
item.description: An opportunity to join the staff of The Economist

item.title: The Rape of Venezuela
item.description: Venezuela’s oil production is rising, yet its currency is depreciating, inflation is accelerating, and foreign exchange remains scarce. The disconnect reflects a political economy built on private dealmaking and a mutually beneficial alliance between ruling elites and US President Donald Trump’s acolytes.

item.title: There Are Only Four Great Powers
item.description: An era of great-power competition has started—but not all would-be competitors qualify.

item.title: 2026 Has Been a Wild Ride for Southeast Asian Stock Markets
item.description: Transparency concerns and the turmoil in global energy markets have created an unusual volatility in the region's capital markets.

item.title: Iran Embraces a Forever War
item.description: Tehran’s new strategic calculus.

item.title: Hezbollah’s Trap for Israel
item.description: The stark choice between occupation and disarmament.

item.title: Why Global Press Freedom Rankings Struggle with Singapore
item.description: The city-state's realities fit awkwardly into an index that draws a firm line between "free" and "unfree" media landscapes.

item.title: The Next Challenge Facing the US-Philippine Alliance, and How They Plan to Overcome It
item.description: This year's Balikatan exercise oriented itself more closely than ever to the Taiwan Strait -- and the possibility of a future conflict over the island.

item.title: Indian PM Hosts Head of Myanmar’s Military Government in New Delhi
item.description: New Delhi has defended its decision to engage with Min Aung Hlaing, who led the coup against Myanmar's democratically elected government in 2021.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: How the character of war has changed
item.description: Shashank Joshi looks back on eight years as defence editor

item.title: Philippine Senator Estrada Surrenders After Anti-graft Court Orders His Arrest
item.description: Jose “Jinggoy” Estrada claims that the charges of plunder are a politically motivated outgrowth of the political feud between the Marcos and Duterte clans.

item.title: An increasingly risky bet for Democrats in Maine
item.description: Graham Platner looks like party’s best chance of unseating Susan Collins. His scandals may get in the way
